mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to manage clusters across various infrastructure environments. The key trade-off is the complexity of setup versus the automation and consistency it provides for cluster lifecycle management.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for installation.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for Cluster API to function properly, as they define the custom resources used by the operator.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the necessary CRDs for Cluster API.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API controller
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API controller using Helm, which manages the lifecycle of clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

```

> Ensure you are installing in the kube-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource that defines a new cluster to be managed by Cluster API.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProviderCluster' with your actual infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> You should see the Cluster API controller pod running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step will remove Cluster API and all associated resources. Be aware that this will lead to data loss for any clusters managed by Cluster API.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-crds.yaml

```

> This command removes the Cluster API controller.
> This command removes the CRDs associated with Cluster API.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your cluster resources. This step will upgrade the Cluster API controller and verify the installation.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o yaml > clusters-backup.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup existing cluster resources.
> This command upgrades the Cluster API controller.
> Verify that the Cluster API controller pod is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD has not been installed or is not available.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-crds.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'context deadline exceeded'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually means that the Cluster API controller is not responding, possibly due to resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check the resource allocation for the controller pod and ensure it has sufficient CPU and memory.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to create cluster: infrastructure provider not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified infrastructure provider is not installed or configured correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the correct infrastructure provider CRDs and controllers are installed.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to connect to the server: dial tcp <IP>:<PORT>: connect: connection refused'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Kubernetes API server is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Check the Kubernetes cluster status and ensure that the API server is running.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved error handling.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old API version for Cluster resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to migrate your Cluster resources to the new API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your clusters with their statuses.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-cluster-infra
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and the necessary tools installed.
